exemplar.title: "Optimizing Airline Ticket Overselling for Profit Maximization"

detailedExemplarData.title: "Optimizing Airline Ticket Overselling for Profit Maximization"

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear introduction stating aims and assumptions
Logical flow with distinct sections and effective headings
Concise transition into sensitivity analysis guiding the reader
weaknesses: Formatting errors (garbled tables, missing captions) interrupt the logical thread
Redundant content such as duplicate columns and stray symbols
Truncated sentences in methodology weaken clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and well organized, with a recognisable structure (introduction, body, conclusion) and generally logical development. However, recurring formatting flaws, interruptions in narrative flow and some unnecessary repetition prevent it from reaching the top band. Overall, this merits a 3/4.

strengths: Mostly consistent and correct use of standard notation for distributions and expectations
Effective use of tables and graphs to illustrate model behaviour
Logical linkage between assumptions, mathematical model and interpretation
weaknesses: Occasional mislabelling of figures and unlabeled axes reduce interpretability
Minor notation inconsistencies (e.g. âE(c)â vs expected cost)
Key formulas (pmf, maxâfunction for compensation) are implied rather than explicitly stated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and multiple representations are used effectively, and communication remains clear overall despite some slips in labelling and precision. The work fits well into band 3 (3/4) for Mathematical Communication.

strengths: Choice of a real-world airline overselling problem shows genuine independent thinking
Justification of assumptions and choice of parameter scenarios reflects a personal approach
Extension to alternative aircraft sizes demonstrates initiative
weaknesses: Mathematical methods follow a standard template without novel techniques
Engagement, though clear, lacks the innovative spark required for the highest level
Opportunities to incorporate real data or design experiments were not pursued
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is significant evidence of independent thinking, personal voice and initiative, but the approach remains within conventional boundaries. This aligns with band 2 (2/3) for Personal Engagement.

strengths: Reflects on model limitations (constant show-up probability, normal approximation)
Links numerical results to economic interpretation (profit drop past optimal s)
Provides clear evaluation of how parameter changes affect outcomes
weaknesses: Depth of reflection is surface-level and does not probe tail risks or confidence intervals
Critical analysis stops at restating limitations without linking back to results
Future implications are mentioned briefly without concrete development
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission contains meaningful reflection and thoughtful analysis, but it falls short of substantial critical depth and comprehensive evaluation. It therefore fits band 2 (2/3) for Reflection.

strengths: Appropriate application of the binomial distribution to model show-ups
Correctly structured profit equation linking revenue and expected compensation
Systematic tabulation of outcomes across varying s supports exploration of model behaviour
weaknesses: Omission of the max(â,0) function for compensation leads to flawed cost calculation
Sign-convention errors in expected-value computations indicate partial understanding
No use of higher-level techniques (e.g. calculus optimisation or formal normal approximation)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics used is relevant and applied correctly at a basic level, supporting the investigationâs aims. However, notable inaccuracies and limited sophistication prevent the work from reaching higher bands. This corresponds to band 3 (3/6) for Use of Mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations link assumptions â model â numeric results â interpretation in a reasonably logical way. Occasional mis-labelling and missing figure references impede clarity, but overall communication remains clear enough for band 3 rather than the limited clarity of band 2.

The student demonstrates some understanding; computations of expected passengers and profit are generally correct, yet the cost formula omits absolute value and the interpretation of negative expectation is flawed. These errors indicate partial understanding with notable inaccuracies, matching band 2.

The student supplies equations, tabular data and several graphs to illustrate profit behaviour under varying parameters. Despite some formatting corruption, the representations complement each other and aid understanding, satisfying the rubric description of âeffective useâ (band 3).

The exploration follows a recognisable flow: introduction, methodology, modelling, results, sensitivity tests and conclusion. Each section generally leads on from the previous one, so a reader can trace the argument without major leaps. Nevertheless, several interruptions occur (e.g. corrupted table rows, missing figure captions), so the logical thread occasionally weakens; hence the work is coherent but not polished enough for the top band.

Adding scenarios for different seat prices, compensation levels and aircraft sizes shows good initiative. However, the extensions remain straightforward and do not introduce new mathematical techniques, placing creativity solidly in band 2.

Results are interpreted (e.g., steep fall in profit past s = 29) and linked to economic rationale. Nonetheless, the analysis never probes why expected value may underestimate risk or explores confidence intervals, so the depth corresponds to a thoughtful but not insightful level.

The student comments on how changing parameters alters the optimal oversell quantity and notes model limitations, providing a clear but not exhaustive evaluation. Future implications are mentioned only briefly, fitting the band-2 description.

The student reflects on limitations such as constant show-up probability and mentions potential normal-approximation alternatives. The reflection goes beyond a token statement, yet it remains surface-level and brief, so it is âmeaningfulâ rather than âsubstantial.â

Standard notation for random variables, parameters and expected value is used and generally correct. Minor slips occur (e.g. âE(c)â instead of an expected cost function and inconsistent use of n vs. 555+s), but symbols are explained and readable throughout, so the use is âmostly consistentâ rather than flawless.

The investigation is clearly the studentâs own: assumptions are justified, parameter values chosen and sensitivity tests performed. This reflects a personal approach, but it does not reach the highly creative or distinctive level required for band 3.

Most material relates directly to the research question, but the write-up contains noticeable redundancy (duplicate revenue-profit columns, stray Greek letters) and several long narrative passages that add little mathematical value. This places the work in the "mostly relevant with some unnecessary elements" descriptor rather than the concise descriptor of band 3.

Binomial expectation, revenue-cost modelling and sensitivity checks are relevant and applied correctly at a basic level. However, the model ignores the distribution tail crucial to bumping risk, so the mathematics is appropriate but not yet âeffectiveâ or âcomprehensive.â

Selecting an authentic airline-management problem and constructing a simple profit model shows genuine independent thinking, but the mathematics follows a textbook approach without major innovation. Thus evidence of independence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstanding.â

There is a clear introduction that states aims and assumptions, a body that develops the mathematics and a concluding evaluation. Headings and sub-headings guide the reader effectively. However, formatting flaws (overlaid symbols, repeated columns) detract from the clarity expected for band 4, so the strand is best described as âwell organised with clear structureâ (band 3).

Content (binomial distribution, expected value) sits squarely within the SL/HL syllabus but does not leverage higher-level skills (e.g., calculus optimisation, normal approximation worked through). Hence the mathematics is mostly, but not fully, at the expected HL level.

annotations.comment: The title clearly states the investigationâs focus on profit-maximizing overselling, establishing immediate context and engagement.
The personal anecdote about United Airlines overselling vividly demonstrates the studentâs engagement and contextual understanding.
The introduction contains lengthy narrative that distracts from the mathematical purpose; consider condensing the anecdote to maintain conciseness.
The methodology paragraph is truncated at âonly two possible outcomes,â leaving the reader uncertain; ensure full sentences and complete descriptions.
The justification for using the binomial distribution is clear and linked directly to the show-up/no-show context.
Assuming exactly 555 seats without discussing variability in aircraft capacity limits the generalizability; reflect on how this choice affects broader applicability.
List assumptions with consistent numbering and provide brief rationale for each to improve organization and clarity.
Using $$X\sim B(s,p)$$ is appropriate and communicates the distribution model clearly.
Defining X as âexcess numberâ without specifying that X counts all show-ups can confuse interpretation; explicitly relate X to the binomial count.
Include the pmf formula $$P(X=k)=\binom{s}{k}p^k(1-p)^{s-k}$$ to enhance mathematical communication of the binomial model.
Why was p chosen at the upper bound of 0.95 rather than a midpoint? Clarify selection criteria to support reflection on parameter choice.
The profit equation $$\text{Profit}=st - c\cdot E(X)$$ is correctly structured, linking revenue and expected compensation.
The expression $$E(X)-555$$ is applied without restricting to positive values; this omits the necessary max(â,0) function for compensation.
Clarify that compensation only applies when $$X>555$$ by writing $$\max(E(X)-555,0)$$ to improve precision.
Numerous formatting errors (overlines, stray symbols) in the table impede readability and logical flow; clean up entries for clarity.
Remove extraneous formatting and align headings in the table to improve conciseness and ensure all data are immediately interpretable.
The tableâs systematic variation of s values with computed outcomes effectively supports the exploration of model behavior.
The interpretation correctly notes that negative expected excess prevents compensation costs, demonstrating thoughtful analysis.
The claim âwhen s=0, E(X)=0â conflicts with earlier calculation of E(X)â555=â27.75; check sign conventions and formula application.
Consider discussing how using expected value underestimates tail risk, deepening reflection on model limitations.
Figure 2âs axes are unlabeled and lack units, which undermines interpretability of profit trends over s.
Add a descriptive caption linking Figure 2 directly to the conclusion to reinforce coherence and guide the reader.
Plotting profits against s in Figure 3 effectively communicates the optimum visually, aiding comprehension.
The summary table on page 8 contains garbled characters, which distracts from clear presentation; correct the entries.
The transition text into sensitivity analysis is concise and helps the reader anticipate the next exploration phase.
The sensitivity discussion conflates compensation and price effects in a single narrative; consider separate subsections for clarity.
Quantify how the profit slope changes under different assumptions to deepen critical reflection on sensitivity results.
Including the scenario of reduced aircraft size (s=16) shows strong personal initiative and exploration of model variations.
The conclusion restates findings without critically evaluating how model assumptions impact the optimum; deepen analysis of limitations.
Extend the conclusion with ideas for incorporating variable show-up rates or classâbased pricing to suggest future model enhancements.
The limitations section acknowledges use of expected value but fails to link how this might shift the optimal s; connect reflections back to results.
Propose specific experiments or data sourcesâfor example, route-based no-show statisticsâto deepen personal engagement and planning.
The recommendation to use normal approximation demonstrates awareness of alternative mathematical tools and broadens methodological perspective.
